name: The Exile - Covarrubias Loop
name_original: Parque Natural Sabinares de Arlanza - La Yecla — circular desde Covarrubias por el Camino del Cid Cicloturista
description: Cycle a difficult 32.4-mile (52.2 km) route through ancient juniper forests and the Desfiladero de la Yecla gorge near Covarrubias.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Covarrubias features routes through the Arlanza River valley, characterized by wooded areas and diverse natural features. The region includes the Sabinares de Arlanza - La Yecla Natural Park, offering varied terrain and views. Cyclists will encounter routes that traverse rural landscapes and surrounding hills, with the Alto de Peña Alta reaching 1078 meters. The area provides a network of paths suitable for exploring its historical and natural environment. Best touring cycling routes around Covarrubias The most popular touring cycling route is Santo Domingo de Silos from Covarrubias — Parque Natural Sabinares de Arlanza - La Yecla — circular, a 32.0 miles (51.6 km) trail that takes 4 hours 7 minutes to complete. This difficult route traverses the Sabinares de Arlanza - La Yecla Natural Park. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is The Exile - Covarrubias Loop, a difficult 32.4 miles (52.2 km) path. This route offers views of the rural landscapes surrounding Covarrubias. Local touring cyclists also love the Alto de Peña Alta (1078 m) – View of Peña de Carazo loop from Covarrubias, a 25.3 miles (40.7 km) trail leading through the Alto de Peña Alta area, often completed in about 2 hours 50 minutes. Touring cycling around Covarrubias is defined by the Arlanza River valley, the Sabinares de Arlanza - La Yecla Natural Park, and historical village backdrops. The network offers options for different ability levels, including challenging climbs. The routes in Covarrubias are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 5 reviews. More than 129 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Covarrubias's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Covarrubias?
A: There are 12 touring cycling routes around Covarrubias. While most are considered difficult, there is one moderate route available for those seeking a less challenging ride.

Q: Are there any easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: The touring cycling routes around Covarrubias are generally challenging, with 11 out of 12 routes rated as difficult. There is one moderate route, but no easy routes are currently listed for touring cyclists. The terrain often includes varied landscapes and hills, making it more suitable for experienced riders.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while cycling in Covarrubias?
A: You can expect to cycle through the picturesque Arlanza River valley, characterized by its extensively wooded areas, including Spanish Juniper. Routes also traverse the diverse Mediterranean landscape of the Sabinares de Arlanza - La Yecla Natural Park, offering varied terrain and stunning vistas of surrounding blue-tinged peaks.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes around Covarrubias?
A: Yes, many routes are circular. For example, you can explore the natural park on the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1030173701/santo-domingo-de-silos-from-covarrubias-parque-natural-sabinares-de-arlanza-la-yecla-circular" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santo Domingo de Silos from Covarrubias — Parque Natural Sabinares de Arlanza - La Yecla — circular&lt;/a&gt; route, or enjoy the rural scenery on &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027732160/the-exile-covarrubias-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Exile - Covarrubias Loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or viewpoints along the touring cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer access to significant landmarks and viewpoints. You can cycle towards &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3568119" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de Peña Alta (1078 m)&lt;/a&gt; for panoramic views, or explore the historic village center. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14938559" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de Peña Alta (1078 m) – View of Peña de Carazo loop from Covarrubias&lt;/a&gt; route specifically highlights these scenic spots.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Covarrubias?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 5 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, the beautiful rural landscapes, and the opportunity to explore both natural parks and historical sites. Over 129 touring cyclists have used komoot to discover the routes here.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through historical sites or charming villages?
A: Absolutely. Covarrubias itself is a 'Conjunto Histórico-Artístico' with traditional Castilian architecture. Routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24549943" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Casa de Doña Sancha – Historic Center of Covarrubias loop from Covarrubias&lt;/a&gt; allow you to cycle past historical monuments such as the Torreón de Fernán González and the Gothic Colegiata de San Cosme y San Damián, or through the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4659990" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Historic Center of Covarrubias&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Covarrubias?
A: While specific weather data isn't provided, the region's Mediterranean landscape suggests pleasant cycling conditions during spring and autumn. These seasons typically offer milder temperatures, making long rides more comfortable and allowing you to fully appreciate the natural beauty of the Arlanza River valley and the Sabinares de Arlanza - La Yecla Natural Park.

Q: Are there any particularly long touring cycling routes for endurance riders?
A: Yes, several routes offer significant distances. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1030173701/santo-domingo-de-silos-from-covarrubias-parque-natural-sabinares-de-arlanza-la-yecla-circular" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santo Domingo de Silos from Covarrubias — Parque Natural Sabinares de Arlanza - La Yecla — circular&lt;/a&gt; route is approximately 51.6 km (32.0 miles), and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027732160/the-exile-covarrubias-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Exile - Covarrubias Loop&lt;/a&gt; is around 52.2 km (32.4 miles), both providing challenging rides for endurance cyclists.

Q: Can I find routes that offer views of the Alto de Peña Alta?
A: Yes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31848228" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Historic Center of Covarrubias – Alto de Peña Alta (1078 m) loop from Covarrubias&lt;/a&gt; is a great option. This route specifically takes you towards the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3568119" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de Peña Alta&lt;/a&gt;, which at 1078 meters, provides breathtaking panoramic views of the surrounding blue-tinged peaks and the natural park.

Q: Are there any natural features like rivers or caves to see while cycling?
A: The region is rich in natural features. Covarrubias is nestled in the Arlanza River valley, so many routes will follow or offer views of the river. The name Covarrubias itself is believed to originate from 'reddish caves' in the area, hinting at interesting geological formations. While not directly on a cycling route, Fuente Azul, a natural spring, is also nearby in Hortigüela.
tips: Impressive statue of El Cid erected in 2008 and founded by the residents of Mecerreyes, it reaches 7m in height if we count the extension of the spear.

Lara de los Infantes is a small town in the province of Burgos. It has a Romanesque church, the Church of Our Lady of the Nativity, and, on top of a hill, are the ruins of a medieval castle. Legend has it that Fernán González, the first independent count of Castile, was born and grew up in this castle.
Available benches, water source and place to make a barbecue. Possible camping.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: This circular route, covering approximately 52.2 km, is generally completed in about 3 hours and 12 minutes of active cycling. However, allow extra time to explore the natural and historical sights along the way, such as the Statue of El Cid in Mecerreyes or the village of Lara de los Infantes.

faqs.question: What is the starting point for this cycling route, and where can I park?
faqs.answer: The route is a circular one starting from the charming medieval town of Covarrubias. You should be able to find parking within or on the outskirts of Covarrubias, which is well-known for its historical appeal. As it's a popular destination, arriving earlier in the day is advisable, especially during peak seasons.

faqs.question: What is the terrain and difficulty level of this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as difficult and requires very good fitness. The terrain is intensely rural and mountainous, with significant hills and sections at elevations above 1,000 meters. Surfaces vary greatly, including asphalt on secondary roads, untarmacked rural roads (generally good but can be stony), natural tracks, greenways, and narrow, irregular trails. Some gravel sections can be very exhausting, potentially requiring you to push your bike.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery and natural features can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers a rich blend of natural and cultural landscapes. You'll cycle through the Parque Natural Sabinares de Arlanza, home to one of the world's largest juniper forests, and experience the dramatic Desfiladero de la Yecla gorge. The broader Camino del Cid section traverses a Castilian landscape of rolling hills and cultivated cereal fields, dotted with small, historic towns.

faqs.question: What wildlife might I encounter in the Parque Natural Sabinares de Arlanza?
faqs.answer: The Parque Natural Sabinares de Arlanza is a significant ecological area, particularly known for its raptor population. Keep an eye out for the majestic Iberian imperial eagle and over a hundred pairs of griffon vultures, which nest in the large limestone cliffs of the area. The ancient juniper forests also support diverse flora and fauna.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best times to cycle this route are typically spring (April to June) and autumn (September to October). During these seasons, the weather is generally milder and more pleasant for cycling, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the colder, potentially wetter conditions of winter. The natural landscapes are also particularly vibrant during these periods.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: While specific regulations for dogs on the cycling sections of the Camino del Cid Cicloturista are not explicitly stated, generally, dogs are permitted on trails in natural parks in Spain, often requiring them to be on a leash. Given the rural nature of the route and potential for wildlife, keeping your dog leashed is recommended for their safety and to respect the natural environment.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay an entrance fee to cycle through the Parque Natural Sabinares de Arlanza?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need a special permit or to pay an entrance fee to cycle through the Parque Natural Sabinares de Arlanza - La Yecla. Natural parks in Spain are generally open to the public for recreational activities. However, always respect park regulations, such as staying on marked paths and not disturbing wildlife.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route incorporates sections of several other trails. Notably, it follows parts of the Camino del Cid Cicloturista (GR 160 - Camino del Cid BTT) and the historic El Destierro. You will also encounter segments of the Vía Verde Santander-Mediterráneo and the Camino de Santiago de la Lana.

faqs.question: What are some notable historical or cultural sites along the route?
faqs.answer: Beyond the natural beauty, the route is rich in history. You'll start in Covarrubias, a beautiful medieval town. The route also passes near Santo Domingo de Silos, famous for its Romanesque monastery. The entire Camino del Cid traces a historical path, offering a unique immersion into Spain's medieval past with numerous small towns and historical sites.

faqs.question: What type of bicycle is most suitable for this route?
faqs.answer: Given the varied and sometimes challenging terrain, a touring bicycle, hybrid bike, or even a gravel bike would be most suitable. While some sections are paved, the route includes untarmacked rural roads, natural tracks, and potentially stony or irregular paths. A road bike might struggle on some of the rougher gravel sections.

waypoints.name: Statue of El Cid in Mecerreyes
